questions.q: In IEEE 754 double precision, machine epsilon $\varepsilon_M$ is closest to which value?
questions.explain: Doubles use a 52-bit mantissa, so $\varepsilon_M = 2^{-52}\approx 2.22\times 10^{-16}$. Single precision (24-bit) gives $2^{-23}\approx 1.19\times 10^{-7}$; the other values are unrelated to the format.

questions.q: A problem is ill-conditioned but the algorithm is backward-stable. What can you conclude about the computed result?
choices: It will be accurate to machine precision
It is the exact solution to a slightly perturbed problem; accuracy depends on the condition number
It is unusable — backward stability is meaningless without forward stability
It is exactly the true solution, since the algorithm is stable
questions.explain: Backward stability means $\hat{y}=f(x+\delta x)$ with $\delta x$ small. The forward error is then bounded by $\kappa(\text{problem})\cdot \|\delta x\|$: a stable algorithm on an ill-conditioned problem still loses digits, and that's not the algorithm's fault.

questions.q: If the relative roundoff per operation is $\varepsilon_M$ and you sum $n=10^6$ floats naively, the worst-case relative error is bounded by approximately $n\varepsilon_M$. With $\varepsilon_M=2^{-52}\approx 2.22\times 10^{-16}$, what is $n\varepsilon_M$?
questions.explain: $10^6 \times 2.22\times 10^{-16} = 2.22\times 10^{-10}$. Kahan summation tightens this to $O(\varepsilon_M)$ regardless of $n$ by carrying a running compensation term.

questions.q: Newton's method applied to $f(x)=x^2-2$ starting from $x_0=1$. What is $x_2$?
questions.explain: Newton: $x_{n+1}=x_n - (x_n^2-2)/(2x_n) = (x_n+2/x_n)/2$. From $x_0=1$: $x_1=(1+2)/2=1.5$, then $x_2=(1.5+4/3)/2=17/12\approx 1.41667$. Quadratic convergence — three iterations get you to machine precision.

questions.q: Bisection on $[0,1]$ with tolerance $10^{-6}$ requires roughly how many iterations?
questions.explain: After $n$ steps the interval length is $2^{-n}$. We need $2^{-n}<10^{-6}$, i.e. $n > \log_2(10^6)\approx 19.93$, so $n=20$. Bisection's linear convergence with rate $1/2$ means each step buys exactly one bit.

questions.q: What is the order of convergence of the secant method near a simple root?
questions.explain: The secant error recursion $e_{n+1}\sim C e_n e_{n-1}$ leads to $p^2 = p+1$, whose positive root is the golden ratio $\phi=(1+\sqrt 5)/2\approx 1.618$. It's slower than Newton's $p=2$ but trades the derivative evaluation for cheaper iterations.

questions.q: Composite trapezoid rule on $n$ panels has error scaling as $h^2$ where $h=(b-a)/n$. If doubling $n$ shrinks the error by what factor?
questions.explain: Halving $h$ shrinks $h^2$ by $4$. So each doubling of $n$ buys two bits of accuracy. Simpson's $O(h^4)$ buys four bits per doubling.

questions.q: Gauss–Legendre quadrature with $n$ nodes integrates polynomials of degree $\le ?$ exactly.
questions.explain: Choosing the $n$ nodes as zeros of the $n$-th Legendre polynomial buys an extra $n$ degrees of exactness over a generic $n$-point quadrature, giving exactness up to degree $2n-1$. This is optimal: no $n$-point rule integrates all polynomials of degree $2n$ exactly.

questions.q: Simpson's rule is exact for polynomials of degree up to:
questions.explain: By construction Simpson's rule integrates parabolas exactly, but a symmetry argument bumps the exactness to cubics: the cubic remainder is odd about the midpoint and integrates to zero. The error term is $-(b-a)h^4 f^{(4)}(\xi)/180$.

questions.q: If $A$ is symmetric positive definite, which factorization is the natural choice and what is its leading cost?
questions.explain: Cholesky exploits SPD structure to halve LU's cost and avoid pivoting. It also fails fast: a non-positive pivot signals that $A$ is not SPD, useful as a numerical SPD test.

questions.q: If $\kappa(A)=10^8$ and you solve $Ax=b$ in IEEE double precision ($\varepsilon_M\approx 10^{-16}$), the relative error in $x$ is bounded by roughly $\kappa(A)\varepsilon_M$. Enter this bound (a number).
questions.explain: $\kappa(A)\,\varepsilon_M = 10^8\cdot 10^{-16}=10^{-8}$. Rule of thumb: you lose $\log_{10}\kappa(A)$ digits of accuracy. Conjugate gradient sees a related but weaker dependence: convergence rate $\sim (\sqrt\kappa-1)/(\sqrt\kappa+1)$.

questions.q: Conjugate gradient on an SPD system with condition number $\kappa$ converges in roughly how many iterations to a fixed tolerance?
questions.explain: The CG error decays as $((\sqrt\kappa-1)/(\sqrt\kappa+1))^k$, so reducing the error by a fixed factor needs $O(\sqrt\kappa)$ steps. This is exponentially better than Jacobi/Gauss–Seidel's $O(\kappa)$ — preconditioning is the standard way to shrink $\kappa$.

questions.q: Which finite difference approximates $f'(x)$ with error $O(h^2)$ rather than $O(h)$?
questions.explain: Taylor expansion: $f(x\pm h)=f(x)\pm h f'(x) + \tfrac{h^2}{2}f''(x)\pm\tfrac{h^3}{6}f'''(x)+\cdots$. The central difference cancels the $f''$ term, leaving $f'(x)+\tfrac{h^2}{6}f'''(x)+\cdots$. The other one-sided differences keep the $f''(x)h/2$ leading error.

questions.q: An explicit FD scheme for the heat equation $u_t=u_{xx}$ is stable when:
choices: Always — explicit schemes are unconditionally stable
questions.explain: Von Neumann analysis on $u^{n+1}_j=u^n_j + (\Delta t/\Delta x^2)(u^n_{j+1}-2u^n_j+u^n_{j-1})$ gives an amplification factor whose modulus stays $\le 1$ iff $\Delta t/\Delta x^2\le 1/2$. Cross that and tiny errors blow up exponentially. Implicit (Crank–Nicolson) schemes lift the restriction at the cost of a linear solve per step.

questions.q: For the wave equation $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$ with $c=2$ and $\Delta x=0.1$, the CFL condition $c\Delta t/\Delta x\le 1$ caps $\Delta t$. What is the largest stable $\Delta t$?
questions.explain: $\Delta t\le \Delta x/c = 0.1/2 = 0.05$. The condition encodes that the numerical domain of dependence must contain the analytic one (the cone with slope $1/c$); otherwise information that should reach a node is missed by the stencil.

questions.q: In the FEM Galerkin formulation for $-\Delta u=f$ on $\Omega$ with $u=0$ on $\partial\Omega$, the stiffness matrix entry $K_{ij}$ equals:
questions.explain:
Multiply $-\Delta u=f$ by $\varphi_j$ and integrate by parts: $\int
abla u\cdot
abla\varphi_j = \int f\varphi_j$. Substituting $u=\sum c_i\varphi_i$ produces $K_{ij}=\int
abla\varphi_i\cdot
abla\varphi_j$ and the load vector $b_j=\int f\varphi_j$. The first option $\int\varphi_i\varphi_j$ is the mass matrix, used for time-dependent problems.

questions.q: With piecewise-linear hat basis on a mesh of size $h$, Céa's lemma gives the a priori estimate $\|u-u_h\|_{H^1}\le ?$
questions.explain: Céa's lemma + linear interpolation error in $H^1$ gives $\|u-u_h\|_{H^1}\le Ch\|u\|_{H^2}$. The $L^2$ estimate is one order better: $\|u-u_h\|_{L^2}\le Ch^2\|u\|_{H^2}$ via Aubin–Nitsche duality. Higher-order ($P^k$) elements scale as $h^k$ in $H^1$.

questions.q: Which property of the FEM stiffness matrix $K$ for $-\Delta$ with Dirichlet BCs makes conjugate gradient an excellent solver?
choices: $K$ is sparse, symmetric, and positive definite
questions.explain: Each $\varphi_i$ has compact support, so $K_{ij}\ne 0$ only when supports overlap — sparse. Symmetry is immediate from the symmetric bilinear form. Positivity: $v^\top K v=\int|\nabla(\sum v_i\varphi_i)|^2\ge 0$ with equality only at $v=0$ (Poincaré on $H^1_0$). SPD + sparse is exactly the regime where CG with multigrid preconditioning shines.
